coredata.dc:title: Effect of addition of carbon dots to the frying oils on oxidative stabilities and quality changes of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Linlin
Zhang, Min
Wang, Haixiang
Devahastin, Sakamon
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and noted to exhibit potent scavenging activities against DPPH·, ·OH, and O2 ·- radicals. Addition of CDs to frying oil as a means to improve oxidative stability and minimize quality changes of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage was investigated in comparison with the use of tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) and carnosic acid (CA). Compared with the control sample, 0.05% CDs significantly reduced thiobarbituric acid reactive substances value, carbonyl and total volatile basic nitrogen contents of fried meatballs. Both lipid and protein oxidation inhibition capabilities of CDs were higher than those of 0.05% CA but lower than those of 0.02% TBHQ. Total sensory score of sample with CDs (7.1 ± 0.06) was significantly higher than those of the control (4.7 ± 0.03) and sample with TBHQ (6.4 ± 0.04). CDs could delay oxidation of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage and can well serve as an alternative antioxidant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidants
Flavor
Lipid oxidation
Physicochemical properties
Protein oxidation
Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of free radical scavenging activities of CDs
Preparation of meatballs and frying process
Determination of oxidative stability and quality parameters
Free radical scavenging abilities of CDs
Oxidative stability and quality of fried meatballs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of the prepared CDs. (a) TEM image, (b) Size distribution histograms, (c) FTIR spectra, (d) Raman spectra, (e) UV–Visible absorption spectra (blue line), PL emission and excitation spectra, (f) PL emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Antioxidant activities of CDs against (a) DPPH·, (b) ·OH, and (c) O2 ·- free radicals (insets: absorbance spectra and color changes of mixture solutions at various concentrations of CDs) and possible reaction mechanisms.
Changes of TBARS value in different meatball samples during storage.
Changes of carbonyl content in different meatball samples during storage.
Changes of TVB-N content in different meatball samples during storage.
(a) Electronic nose signals of different fried meatballs, (b) PCA of electronic nose signals of different fried meatballs.
Sensors and sensing types of electronic nose.
Criteria for sensory evaluation of fried meatballs.

row.entry: Pure meatball flavor, no peculiar flavor
Surface is golden brown, uniform in color, and rich in luster
Dense texture, moderate elasticity, mellow meatball taste
Insufficient meatball flavor, slightly peculiar flavor
Surface is golden brown, uneven in color
Normal texture, moderate elasticity, slight meatball taste
No meatball flavor, slight rancid smell
Surface is brown, uneven in color
Loose texture and without elasticity, slight rancid taste

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EC50 values of CDs, TBHQ, CA and VC against various free radicals.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different superscripts within the same row imply that mean values are significantly different (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensory quality scores of meatballs of different groups.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different superscripts within the same column imply that mean values are significantly different (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Effect of addition of carbon dots to the frying oils on oxidative stabilities and quality changes of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Food Processing Research Laboratory, Department of Food Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi, 126 Pracha u-tid Road, Tungkru, Bangkok 10140, Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and noted to exhibit potent scavenging activities against DPPH·, ·OH, and O2 ·- radicals. Addition of CDs to frying oil as a means to improve oxidative stability and minimize quality changes of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage was investigated in comparison with the use of tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) and carnosic acid (CA). Compared with the control sample, 0.05% CDs significantly reduced thiobarbituric acid reactive substances value, carbonyl and total volatile basic nitrogen contents of fried meatballs. Both lipid and protein oxidation inhibition capabilities of CDs were higher than those of 0.05% CA but lower than those of 0.02% TBHQ. Total sensory score of sample with CDs (7.1 ± 0.06) was significantly higher than those of the control (4.7 ± 0.03) and sample with TBHQ (6.4 ± 0.04). CDs could delay oxidation of fried meatballs during refrigerated storage and can well serve as an alternative antioxidant.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) refer to a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with diameters smaller than 10 nm (Li et al., 2018). CDs possess unique morphology and surface functionality with a number of desirable physicochemical properties (De & Karak, 2013), including superior water solubility, photo stability and biocompatibility; CDs are also noted to be non-toxic (Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014). CDs are most commonly prepared via hydrothermal methods. Several raw materials can be used for the preparation. Waste materials, including fruit and vegetable by-products can indeed be used as starting raw materials for the preparation of CDs, making this nanomaterial well fit into the current circular economy trend (Fan, Zhang, Bhandari, & Yang, 2020; Murru, Badía-Laíño, & Díaz-García, 2020; Wang, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2011; Wu, Zhang, Wang, Mothibe, & Chen, 2012). CDs have widely been used in various application areas, including drug delivery, biosensing and bioimaging (Huang et al., 2019).

As for applications in food processing and preservation, CDs have found their use as a potent antimicrobial agent. Our previous studies have demonstrated that CDs exhibit abilities to inactivate yeasts, molds as well as selected bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis. CDs have been noted to help enhance microbial safety of several such products as packaged soy products (Zhao, Zhang, Bhandari, & Bai, 2020) and fresh-cut cucumber (Fan, Zhang, Fan, & Jiang, 2019) without sacrificing quality during storage. Besides their antimicrobial activities, the antioxidant activities of CDs have recently received increasing attention. Murru et al. (2020), for example, prepared CDs from vegetable wastes by a hydrothermal means and illustrated that the obtained CDs had excellent antioxidant activity. In yet another study, Sharma, Das, and Yun (2020) synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from red cabbage, also by a hydrothermal means, and tested the obtained products against DPPH·, ·OH and KMnO4 radicals. Red-cabbage CQDs exhibited outstanding scavenging activities on all radicals that were tested. The antioxidant activities of CDs have been reported to be affected by sp2 hybrid carbon domains, hydrogen donor behavior, electron transport, unpaired electrons caused by vacancies and defects, types of doping elements as well as types of surface functional groups (Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2019).

Lipid oxidation is one of the major reasons for meat product quality losses. Secondary oxidation products such as malondialdehydes (Li, Zhang, & Yu, 2006), isofurans, aldehydes and ketones are known to cause rancidity of meats (Turgut, Işikçi, & Soyer, 2017). Lipid oxidation also adversely affects other sensory characteristics of meat products, including texture, color and appearance, resulting in shorter shelf life of the products. Secondary oxidation products generated through lipid oxidation can as well cause protein oxidation via direct modification with reactive oxygen species (Khan et al., 2020). Similar to lipid oxidation, protein oxidation is a reason for the deterioration of meat product quality; this latter oxidation can result in changes in protein functionality, alterations of texture, solubility and amino acids and may even cause the generation of toxic compounds (Rice-Evans & Burdon, 1993). Oxidation of meat proteins during storage and processing also leads to accumulation of oxidation products, which are exposed to the gastrointestinal tract after being eaten, which may contribute to altering the redox state of intestinal tissues and hence the disturbance of intestinal flora (Xie et al., 2014).

Based on the aforementioned arguments, a means to mitigate oxidation in meat products is of importance. Adding antioxidants is among the simplest and most obvious alternatives to alleviate the oxidation. Synthetic antioxidants are, however, not preferred, especially by health-conscious consumers. Naturally derived antioxidants have therefore emerged as alternatives to their synthetic counterparts. Some of these natural antioxidants nevertheless exhibit specific flavors and may interfere with the original flavor of a product. Searching for alternative natural antioxidants, which would not interfere with the flavor (and aroma) of a product and at the same time exhibit potent antioxidant activities is therefore of interest to the food industry. Due to their antimicrobial and antioxidant activities, CDs could serve this purpose. Limited studies are, however, available on the use of CDs to preserve the quality of processed meat products, especially those with high fat content, during storage.

In this study, CDs were first synthesized and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transfer infrared (FTIR), Raman, UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopies, with the attempt to elucidate their antioxidant mechanisms. The antioxidant activities of the CDs were assessed against DPPH·, ·OH and O2 ·- radicals. CDs were then used to mitigate oxidation of fried meatballs, which were used as a model processed meat product, during refrigerated storage. Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) and carnosic acid (CA) were used as control antioxidants for comparative purpose. By monitoring changes in thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) value, protein carbonyl content, total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content and sensory characteristics, the ability of CDs to alleviate oxidation and maintaining the quality of fried meatballs were evaluated, so as to provide a theoretical basis for the development and utilization of CDs as an alternative health-friendly antioxidant.

Materials for the preparation of meatballs, including lean pork meat, back pork fat, salt, onion powder, ginger powder, soy sauce, sugar, white wine, corn starch soybean oil were provided by Yechun Food Co., Ltd. (Yangzhou, China). Banana was purchased from a supermarket in Wuxi, China. Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) was purchased from Zelang Biotech Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China), while carnosic acid (CA) was provided by Hunan Xianwei Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China). Vitamin C was purchased from Tianjin Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

CDs were prepared as suggested by Zhao, Zhang, Bhandari, and Bai (2020). Briefly, 15 g of banana flesh and 40 mL of distilled water were mixed and turned into puree. Thirty mL of banana puree was transferred into a 50-mL hydrothermal vessel; 10 mL of ethanol was added and well mixed. The vessel and its content was placed in a muffle furnace and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. The resulting product was allowed to cool to room temperature (about 25 °C) and centrifuged at 3000 ×g. Supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. The filtrate was purified through a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da) for 1 day. Finally, the dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

TEM images of CDs were acquired using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV. Diameters of the CDs were determined using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Panalytical, Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Worcestershire, UK). FTIR spectra of the CDs were recorded via the use of a spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet, iS10, Waltham, MA, USA), while Raman spectra were obtained using a confocal Raman microscope (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA Jobin Yvon S.A.S, Palaiseau, France). UV–visible absorbance was acquired using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 35, Waltham, MA, USA), while fluorescence spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Fukuoka, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of free radical scavenging activities of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
DPPH radical scavenging assay (Murru et al., 2020) was first conducted. One mL of ethanolic solution of 0.1 mM DPPH was added to 1 mL of CDs at different concentrations (0.5–64 μg/mL). The absorbance of each mixture was measured at 517 nm after allowing the reaction to take place for 20 min in the dark. DPPH scavenging activity of CDs was then calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
% Scavenging capacity =
1 −
A i
/
A 0
× 100 %
where A i refers to the absorbance of the mixture with CDs and A 0 refers to the absorbance of the mixture without CDs.

The scavenging activity of CDs against hydroxyl free radicals (•OH) was determined on the basis of Fenton reaction (Huang et al., 2019). Briefly, 200 μL of FeSO4 solution (7.5 mM) and 600 μL of ethanolic salicylic acid solution (1.8 mM) were mixed with 100 μL of CDs at different concentrations (0.03–4 mg/mL); this was followed by the addition of 200 μL of H2O2 (200 mM). Then, doubly distilled water was added to make up the volume to 2 mL. The absorbance at 510 nm was measured after allowing the reaction to take place for 15 min at 37 °C. The •OH scavenging activity was calculated using Eq. (1).

The scavenging activity of CDs against superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−) was determined using the methods of Bae and Suh (2007) with some modification. Five hundred μL of xanthine (0.4 mM) was mixed with 500 μL of 0.24 mM nitro blue tetrazolium chloride; this was followed by mixing with 1 mL of xanthine oxidase (0.049 unit/mL). Then, 500 μL of CDs at different concentrations (0.016–2 mg/mL) was added. After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the absorbance was measured at 550 nm; the O2 •− scavenging efficiency of CDs was again calculated using Eq. (1). TBHQ, CA and vitamin C were used as comparative antioxidants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of meatballs and frying process
ce:section.ce:para: Lean pork meat and back fat were first diced and then mixed with salt, onion powder, ginger powder, soy sauce, sugar, white wine, corn starch and water. Meatballs were shaped by hand, each weighed 30 ± 0.5 g. A total of 2.4 kg of samples (80 meatballs) from each treatment was prepared. Note that the meatballs were independently prepared with the same formulation and ingredients and all the subsequently described experiments were performed in triplicate.

Meatballs were fried in oil samples with different antioxidants; fried meatballs were divided into 4 groups, i.e., control group (fried in oil without antioxidant), CDs group (fried in oil with 0.05% CDs), TBHQ group (fried in oil with 0.02% TBHQ) and CA group (fried in oil with 0.05% CA). Frying was conducted in a 1.5-L stainless steel fryer (WeighMax, XJ-6 K116, Huanggang, China). The frying temperature was 160 ± 5 °C, while the frying time was 4 min in order to reach an internal temperature of around 78 °C. After frying, meatballs were cooled at room temperature for 40 min; internal temperature of the cooled meatballs was 25 °C. The meatballs were then vacuum packed. Twelve samples corresponding to the four groups were used directly for flavor analysis, while others were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 15 days. At each 3-day interval (0, 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 days), 12 meatballs corresponding to the four sample groups were taken out for the analysis of TBARS value as well as carbonyl and TVB-N contents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of oxidative stability and quality parameters

ce:section.ce:para: TBARS value of each fried meatball sample was determined as per the National Standard of the PR China GB 5009.181–2016. Briefly, 5 g of the sample was mixed with 50 mL of trichloroacetic acid (7.5%) and then homogenized at 6000 rpm for 1 min. The homogenate was centrifuged at 3, 000 ×g for 15 min; supernatant was filtered using Whatman filter paper. Five mL of the filtrate was mixed with 5 mL of thiobarbituric acid (0.02 mol/L); the mixture was heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 30 min. After cooling at room temperature of 25 °C for 30 min, the absorbance of the mixture was measured at 532 nm. A standard curve was prepared using 1,1,3,3-tetramethoxypropane at concentrations in the range of 0.01–0.25 μg/mL. The results are expressed as mg malondialdehyde per kg sample.
The protein carbonyl content of each fried meatball sample was determined by the dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) assay as suggested by Rodríguez-Carpena, Morcuende, and Estévez (2011).

The determination of TVB-N content was based on the semi-trace determination of nitrogen as outlined in the National Standard of the PR China GB/T 5009.44–2003. A sample was minced and stirred in 100 mL of ultrapure water for 30 min. The mixture was filtered with a double gauze. The filtrate was mixed with magnesium oxide suspension (10 g/L) and distilled for 6 min. The distilled liquid was adsorbed with boric acid (20 g/L), then titrated with hydrochloric acid (HCl, 0.01 mol/L). TVB-N content was calculated as per the following equation:
(2)
X =
V 1
−
V 2
× 0.01 × 14
M × 5 / 100
× 100
where X is the of TVB-N content (mg/100 g), M is the sample mass (g), V
1 and V
2 are the volumes of the HCl solution used to titrate the sample and the blank control, respectively (mL).

An electronic nose (Ruifen Trading Co., China) was used to analyze the flavor changes of different groups of fried meatballs. A sample (4 g) was placed in a 40-mL sealed bottle, which was then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min to allow the volatile compounds in the headspace to reach an equilibrium. Four such measurements were performed for each sample. The following measurement parameters were employed: detection time of 150 s, gas flow rate of 1 L/min, waiting time of 10 s, cleaning time of 180 s; the sampling method was direct sampling. In order to reduce interferences and errors between the samples and to improve detection sensitivity, the electronic nose was cleaned for at least 40 min prior to any subsequent use. Sensors and sensing types of the electronic nose are listed in Table 1 .

Sensory analyses were conducted on the four sample groups stored for 15 days as well as on the freshly prepared control sample. The sensory assessment team consisted of 20 trained panelists (10 males and 10 females), the ages of which were 20–35 years old. Panelists were trained to identify and assess the tastes of supra-threshold solutions to become familiar with the terms used for the analysis. The solutions used were 20 mmol/L L-lactic acid, 20 mmol/L D-sucrose, 20 mmol/L NaCl, 0.05% tannic acid, 6 mmol/L monosodium L-glutamate and 2 mmol/L caffeine for the sour taste, sweet taste, salty taste, puckering-astringent, umami taste and bitter taste, respectively.

Before the test, three sessions were conducted to familiarize the panelists with the samples. Sensory evaluation was performed in one session. A total of 40 meatballs, 10 meatballs per each of the four sample groups were fried according to the methods described in Section 2.4 and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 15 days. On the day of sensory analysis, 10 meatballs without antioxidants were prepared. Packaged refrigerated meatballs were boiled in water at 100 °C for 8 min. Twenty fried meatballs, 5 meatballs per each of the four sample groups, and 5 freshly prepared meatballs were cut; each meatball was cut into four pieces. Other meatballs used for color and appearance evaluation were not cut. All the samples were kept in an oven at 50 °C until the time of serving. The samples were served in disposable paper plates; the samples were pre-coded with 3 random digit codes and presented to the panelists in random order at a temperature of approximately 40 °C. Five small-piece samples and five uncut meatballs were served to each panelist for the evaluation of flavor, color, taste, and appearance. A 9-point hedonic scale was used to evaluate the samples; the criteria used for the evaluation are listed in Table 2 . When any of the score was lower than 5, the sample was considered unacceptable by the panelists. The sensory test was performed in separated sensory booths under controlled environmental condition (20–22 °C) with white light.

Experimental data are expressed as mean values with standard deviations. Quality data were analyzed with a general linear model considering the treatments (different antioxidants) as a fixed effect, while the replications as a random effect. Differences between mean values were established using Duncan's new multiple range tests at a confidence level of 95%. For sensory analysis, treatments were considered the main effect and the panelists were included in the model as the random effect. The results of the sensory evaluation were analyzed through the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the reported results are the means (with standard deviations) of those scored by the 20 panelists. All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS version 22.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1a), CDs exhibited spherical shape with good dispersibility. Fig. 1b further illustrates that the diameters of CDs were mostly smaller than 1 nm. FTIR spectra of CDs (Fig. 1c) were characterized by the peaks at 3394 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, 1701 cm−1, 1631 cm−1 and 1028 cm−1, which are attributed to C-OH, CH, CO, CC, and CO, respectively. These results indicate that CDs were functionalized with oxygen-containing groups, including carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, which are typically located at the defects or edges of the graphene sheets; these groups are known to be capable of providing the hydrogen atoms (Wang et al., 2019). Therefore, at the unsaturated bonds, CDs as a hydrogen donor have a capability to scavenge free radicals.

Fig. 1d shows the Raman spectra of CDs. The spectra exhibit two major peaks at 1348 and 1580 cm−1 (D peak and G peak); D and G peaks, respectively, represent the disordered structures of sp2 hybrid carbon and in-plane vibration of the sp2 carbon atoms (Lin, Tsai, Dehvari, Huang, & Chang, 2019). It is indeed known that the intensity ratio of these peaks (ID/IG) is related to the degree of surface defects of CDs. The ID/IG radio in our case was around 1.18, indicating that CDs possessed a number of surface defects, which could serve as active sites for neutralizing free radicals (Zhao et al., 2019).

UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 1e) exhibited an absorption peak at 283 nm, induced by the π-π* transition of electron in the conjugated CC band (Du et al., 2020). This indicates that the CDs structure had aromatic sp2 carbon domains (Zhu et al., 2019). Fig. 1e shows the fluorescence spectra, with an emission wavelength of 460 nm and optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm. As the excitation wavelength changed as shown in Fig. 1f, the maximum emission shifted to longer wavelengths with the intensity first increased and then steadily declined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging abilities of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2a, DPPH· radicals had a strong absorption intensity at 517 nm. With increasing CDs concentration, the absorption intensity decreased, indicating a positive correlation between the concentration and free radical scavenging ability of CDs. Such scavenging ability may be related to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on CDs. As shown in the accompanying postulated scavenging mechanisms, neutralization might be due to the DPPH·-H generation via the hydrogen atoms donated by the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Chunduri et al., 2016; Tao, Zhang, & Yu, 2007). Hence, the purple DPPH solution changed its color to yellow (Islam, Zhang, Adhikari, Cheng, & Xu, 2014; Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai, 2017).

·OH radicals were generated by the Fenton reaction and their absorbance was measured at 510 nm (Fig. 2b). Similar to the case of DPPH· radicals, the scavenging ability of CDs aginst ·OH radicals exhibited a concentration-dependent increase. Possible scavenging mechanisms of CDs on ·OH radicals is also given. The functional groups on CDs surface could donate hydrogen atoms to react with ·OH radicals.

Evaluating the absorbance change with the concentration of CDs revealed the capability of CDs to scavenge O2 ·- radicals (Fig. 2c). With an increase in the concentration of CDs from 15.625 μg/mL to 2 mg/mL, the absorbance decreased, while the scavenging ability increased from 6.32% to 88.21%; color of the solution changed from pink to yellow. These results indicate that CDs could scavenge O2 ·- radicals, again in a concentration-dependent manner. Plausible scavenging mechanisms in this case may be the electron transfer from CDs to O2 ·- radicals, leading to the generation of the more stable molecules.

In addition to CDs, the inhibition abilities of TBHQ, CA and vitamin C were also determined. TBHQ is a synthetic antioxidant, which is widely applied in industrial processing and has proved to effectively reduce lipid oxidation and improve the quality of food products and extend their shelf life (Akcan, Estévez, & Serdaroğlu, 2017). However, use of such a synthetic antioxidant has been restricted in Europe and some other countries due to its potential health hazard (Oz & Kaya, 2011). CA is, on the other hand, a natural antioxidant and the most bioactive component of rosemary extract; CA has widely been used in various kinds of foods, including meat products, fish oils and frying oils, to inhibit oxidation and reduce the formation of oxidation products (Urbančič, Kolar, Dimitrijević, Demšar, & Vidrih, 2014). Vitamin C was selected for the present comparative purpose as it is commonly used as a standard antioxidant. The activities of these antioxidants were compared by calculating the concentrations at which free radicals were scavenged by 50% (EC50 value). The lower the EC50 value, the higher the antioxidant activity.

As shown in Table 3 , vitamin C exhibited the lowest EC50 values against DPPH·, ·OH, and O2 ·- radicals and hence possessed the highest antioxidant activity. EC50 values of CDs against DPPH·, ·OH radicals were obviously (P < 0.05) higher than those of TBHQ, but the value against O2 ·- radicals was significantly (P < 0.05) lower than that of TBHQ. EC50 value of CA against DPPH· radicals was significantly (P < 0.05) higher than those of CDs, TBHQ and vitamin C. However, when applied against ·OH, and O2 ·- radicals, CA and its solution flocculated during the reaction; this seriously affected the measurement of the absorbance and resulted in the no detectable value.

Based on the above-mentioned results, the scavenging ability against DPPH· radicals could be ranked as vitamin C > TBHQ > CDs > CA, the scavenging activity against ·OH radicals could be ranked as vitamin C > TBHQ > CDs and the scavenging activity against O2·- radicals could be ranked as vitamin C > CDs > TBHQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stability and quality of fried meatballs

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid oxidation in meat products is usually evaluated by the thiobarbituric acid assay. TBARS value reflects the amount of secondary products (represented by MDA) formed by the oxidation and decomposition of unsaturated fatty acids in meat products. It can be seen in Fig. 3 that the TBARS values of the four sample groups all showed an upward tendency at extended storage time. This is because of the high fat content of the fried meatballs. As the degree of oxidation increased, more secondary products were accumulated, causing the TBARS value to increase. During the whole storage period, the value belonging to the control sample increased most rapidly; the TBARS value reached 1.95 mg MDA/kg after 15 days of storage. Fried meatballs with 0.02% TBHQ experienced the smallest change in the TBARS value; this was followed by the samples with 0.05% CDs and 0.05% CA. TBARS value of the CDs group reached 1.54 mg MDA/kg at the end of the storage period (15 days); the value was significantly (P < 0.05) lower than that of the CA group and significantly (P < 0.05) higher than that of the TBHQ group, indicating that the capability of CDs in delaying lipid oxidation of fried meatballs was higher than CA, but lower than TBHQ.

In addition to lipid oxidation, protein oxidation is another major reason of food quality degradation. This latter oxidation reaction is caused by peptide bond scission and amino acid side chain modification, formation of carbonyl compounds and their derivatives. For this reason, the degree of protein oxidation can be evaluated by measuring the content of carbonyl compounds. Over the entire storage period (Fig. 4 ), the changing trends of the carbonyl content of meatball samples were in agreement with those of the TBARS values. The carbonyl contents of meatballs of different groups were significantly (P < 0.05) lower than that of the control sample, demonstrating that CA, CDs and TBHQ could help inhibit the generation of carbonyl compounds. The value belonging to the CDs group was lower than that belonging to the CA group and higher than that belonging to the TBHQ group, indicating that CDs exhibited stronger protective effect on protein oxidation; the effect was more pronounced than that of CA but less than that of TBHQ.

Addition of antioxidants to the frying oil could not only delay oxidative rancidity of the oil, but also enhance oxidative stability of fried meatballs during storage. During the frying process, antioxidants might have been transferred to the meatballs through the adsorption of oil, thus enhancing the oxidative stability of the fried products. Prabsangob and Benjakul (2019) similarly reported that adding tea catechin derivatives into frying oil could reduce the extents of lipid and protein oxidations in fried fish crackers during subsequent storage.

Considered that both CDs (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2020) and CA (Pavić, Jakovljević, Molnar, & Jokić, 2019) also exhibit certain antibacterial activities, changes in the TVB-N content of the fried meatballs during the storage were investigated. TVB-N content indicates the activities of endogenous enzymes and microorganisms in the muscles of animal-derived foods, which decompose proteins and produce volatile ammonia and alkaline nitrogen-containing substances (Zhao, Jiang, Xu, Wang, & Xu, 2017). The higher the TVB-N content, the lower the freshness of meat products.

After the meatballs were fried at a higher temperature, endogenous enzymes would have been inactivated; alkaline nitrogen-containing substances were therefore mainly produced by the residual microorganisms during the storage (Cachaldora, García, Lorenzo, & García-Fontán, 2013). As shown in Fig. 5 , with extended storage period, the TVB-N contents of all the samples expectedly increased, especially that belonging to the control group. Since CDs and CA exhibit antibacterial properties, the TVB-N contents of the CDs and CA groups were smaller than those of the control sample and TBHQ group; TVB-N content of the CDs group was the smallest. In low-temperature environment, the main spoilage bacteria in vacuum packaging are facultative anaerobes, lactic acid bacteria, whose abilities to decompose proteins are weak (Pexara, Metaxopoulos, & Drosinos, 2002). Therefore, from the perspective of TVB-N content, CDs exhibited the highest antimicrobial efficacy; this was followed by CA.

During frying, a number of volatile compounds (complex mixtures of aldehydes, ketones and acids) are formed due to oil oxidation. The longer the frying time, the more volatile substances accumulate and the more significant the flavor changes of a fried sample experiences. By adding an antioxidant, oxidation reactions can be mitigated, thereby reducing the accumulation of volatile compounds. However, since both CDs and CA have their own specific flavors, adding these antioxidants to the frying oil may affect the flavor of the fried meatballs. Electronic nose was therefore used to evaluate flavor changes of the meatballs after frying.

Fig. 6a shows the flavor response values of different fried meatball samples. The S5, S9 and S10 sensor signal values of the CDs, CA and TBHQ groups were obviously lower than that of the control sample. Note that S5 sensor can detect aromatic compounds, ketones, aldehydes, alcohols, while S9 and S10 sensors can detect aliphatic hydrocarbons and hydrocarbons, respectively. These results indicate that small volatile molecules were generated by oil oxidation during the high-temperature frying process of meatballs, which changed the flavor of the samples. According to the signals generated by the S5 sensor, the value belonging to the TBHQ group was the lowest among the 4 groups of samples, indicating that 0.02% TBHQ exhibited the highest inhibitory effect on the flavor changes of the fried meatballs; this was followed by the CDs group. The signal values of all 14 sensors belonging to the CDs and CA groups did not drastically change, indicating that the specific flavors of CDs and CA did not affect the flavor of the fried meatballs.

Based on the principal component analysis results shown in Fig. 6b, principal component 1 and principal component 2 were 93.6% and 3.2%, respectively. The cumulative contribution was 96.8% and the DI value was negative, demonstrating that the overall information of the samples could be displayed (An, Zhang, Lu, & Zhang, 2006; Wang, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2010; Zhang, Zhang, Fang, & Liu, 2017), but the differences between the 4 groups of samples could not be completely distinguished. The CDs and CA groups exhibited overlapping parts on the plot; these two samples were also noted to be far away from the control group, illustrating that the flavors of the two groups were not significantly different, but were significantly (P < 0.05) different from that of the control sample.

In the present study, sensory characteristics of freshly prepared control samples were considered to be the best; sensory quality deterioration of the other samples after 15 days of storage was then evaluated against the freshly prepared control samples. Note that the goal here was to evaluate the effects of CDs, CA and TBHQ on preserving the sensory characteristics of the meatballs, with the hope of finding the best means to extend the shelf-life of the product undergoing refrigerated storage.

Table 4 shows the sensory quality scores of the four sample groups after 15 days of storage; the scores belonging to the freshly prepared control samples are also listed. The total score of freshly fried meatballs was 8.7 ± 0.05; the samples were noted to exhibit pure meatball flavor, uniform color, dense texture and high surface integrity. The scores belonging to the other samples were significantly (P < 0.05) lower. The total score of the control sample indeed decreased to lower than 5. Such a sample lost the unique aroma of fried meatballs and instead possessed the rancid odor; its characters had indeed deteriorated to become unacceptable.

The total score of the CDs group was 7.1 ± 0.06, which was slightly higher than that of the CA group (P > 0.05). There were no significant differences in the flavor, color, taste and appearance among these two sample groups. The sensory characteristics of the CDs group, including flavor, color and taste were indeed closer to the freshly fried meatballs; its total score was significantly (P < 0.05) higher than those of the TBHQ and control groups. This indicates that the flavor, color and taste of the CDs group were still at a good level and were more acceptable by the panelists. The appearance of all the treated samples was nevertheless quite different from that of the freshly fried sample. This may be because the samples were physically squeezed during the vacuum packaging process, causing the meatballs to deform and subsequently compromised the compactness and integrity of the product.

CDs were successfully prepared from banana via a simple hydrothermal method. CDs exhibited excellent scavenging activities against DPPH·, ·OH and O2 ·- radicals. Addition of only 0.05% CDs could significantly reduce the degrees of lipid and protein oxidations of fried meatballs during 15-day refrigerated storage as indicated by significant reduction in the TBARS value and carbonyl content as compared to the values belonging to the control sample with no added antioxidant. TVB-N content of the fried meatballs was also lower, due most probably to the antimicrobial activity of CDs. Lipid and protein oxidation inhibition capabilities of CDs were noted to be higher than those of 0.05% CA but weaker than those of 0.02% TBHQ. Specific flavor of CDs and CA did not nevertheless affect the flavor of the fried meatballs. As a nano-antioxidant, CDs have potent antioxidant activity and potential to be used in place of alternative natural antioxidant (i.e., 0.05% CA) and even synthetic antioxidant (i.e., 0.02% TBHQ) without compromising the product quality. Studies on the application of CDs to preserve other processed meat products should be conducted, however. Investigation on the impact of CDs on frying oil quality degradation should also be made along with the determination of the relevant cost of using CDs in comparison with that of using TBHQ.

Research and drafting performed by Linlin Zhao. Oversight of the project and field experience were provided by Min Zhang, Haixiang Wang and Sakamon Devahastin. Critical review of the manuscript was performed by Min Zhang, Haixiang Wang and Sakamon Devahastin.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial supports from the China Key Research Program (Contract No. 2017YFD0400501), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180205), Yangzhou City Agricultural Key R&D Program (No.YZ2019034), the 111 Project (BP0719028), Research Innovation Project for Postgraduates in Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX20_1846), Jiangsu Province (China) “Collaborative Innovation Center for Food Safety and Quality Control” Industry Development Program, all of which enabled us to carry out this study.
